title: Key distribution in a distributed computing environment
abstract: A key distribution service operated by a signature authority distributes one-time-use cryptographic keys to one or more delegates that generate digital signatures on behalf of the signature authority. The key distribution service uses a root seed value to generate subordinate seeds. The subordinate seeds are used to generate a set of cryptographic keys. Hashes are generated for each key, and the hashes are arranged into a Merkle tree with a root hash controlled by the signature authority. In response to a request from a delegate, the signature authority provides a subordinate seed to the delegate. The delegate uses the subordinate seed to generate one or more cryptographic keys. The cryptographic keys are used to generate digital signatures which are verifiable up to the root hash of the Merkle tree. Additional subordinate seeds may be distributed to entities by the signature authority when appropriate.
claims: Claims CLAIMS  WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A computer-implemented method, comprising: receiving, at a key distribution service operating within a distributed computing environment and hosted by a signature authority computer system that has authority over a set of one-time-use keys, the set of one-time-use keys including secret portions and public portions, the public portions of the set of one-time-use keys linked to a Merkle tree having a public key of the signature authority computer system at a root the Merkle tree, a request for a number of one-time-use keys from a requester; providing, from the key distribution service, information to the requester that enables the requester to generate a subset of the set of one-time-use keys; and providing, to the requester, from the key distribution service, one or more verification hashes from the Merkle tree that enable the requester to verify a digital signature against the public key at the root of the Merkle tree using the verification hashes, the digital signature generated with a delegated one-time-use cryptographic key selected from the subset of the one-time-use keys. 2. The computer-implemented method of claim 1, further comprising: receiving, at the key distribution service, an additional request from the requester for an additional number of one-time-use keys; and providing, to the requester, from the key distribution service, additional information that reveals an additional subset of the set of one-time-use keys. 3. The computer-implemented method of claim 1, further comprising: generating a seed tree of one or more subordinate seed values from a master seed value; generating the secret portions of the subset of the set of one-time-use keys from seed values at leaf nodes of the seed tree; and wherein the information is a seed value of the one or more subordinate seed values. 4. The computer-implemented method of claim 3, further comprising: generating the public portions of the subset of the set of one-time-use keys by determining cryptographic hashes of the secret portions of the subset of the set of one-time- use keys; and generating one or more of the verification hashes by generating one or more hashes of the public portions of the subset of the set of one-time-use keys. 5. A system, comprising: at least one computing device that includes a processor and a physical memory storing executable instructions that, as a result of being executed, implement a key distribution service that: generates a set of one-time-use cryptographic keys, the set of one-time- use cryptographic keys including secret keys and public keys cryptographically derived from the secret keys; generates, from the public keys, a set of key hashes in a hash tree, a root of the hash tree representing a root public key of a signature authority; selects a subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys to be provided to a delegate authorized to sign on behalf of the signature authority; provides, to the delegate, information from which secret keys associated with the subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys are determinable; and provides verification values that enable the delegate to derive the root public key from the secret keys. 6. The system of claim 5, wherein the key distribution service: generates a master seed value; generates a tree of subordinate seed nodes with the master seed value as the root of the tree, and individual subordinate seed nodes of the tree derived from a cryptographic hash of an individual subordinate seed node's parent seed node; and generates the secret keys from leaf nodes of the tree. 7. The system of claim 6, wherein the key distribution service: identifies a set of subordinate seed nodes from the tree capable of generating the subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys, and not capable of generating other cryptographic keys in the set of one-time-use cryptographic keys; and provides the set of subordinate seed nodes to the delegate. 8. The system of claim 5, wherein the key distribution service: records, in a database maintained by the key distribution service, that the subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys has been assigned to the delegate; and confirms that the subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys has not been assigned to another delegate. 9. The system of claim 5, wherein set of one-time-use cryptographic keys is a set of Lamport signature keys or Winternitz signature keys. 10. The system of claim 5, wherein: the delegate submits a request for one-time-use cryptographic keys to the key distribution service via a network service interface; and the key distribution service provides the secret keys associated with the subset of the one-time-use cryptographic keys in response to the request. 11. The system of claim 5, wherein: the secret keys are randomly generated by the key distribution service; and the public keys are cryptographically derived from the secret keys. 12. The system of claim 5, wherein the key distribution service: determines a first quantity of one-time-use cryptographic keys requested by the delegate; determines a second quantity of one-time-use cryptographic keys in the set of one-time-use cryptographic keys that have not been used to generate a digital signature or provided to a delegate signature authority; and determines that the second quantity is greater than or equal to the first quantity as a condition of providing one-time-use cryptographic keys to the delegate. 13. A non-transitory computer-readable storage medium having stored thereon executable instructions that, as a result of being executed by one or more processors of a computer system, cause the computer system to at least: generate a set of one-time-use keys that include secret key parts and public key parts, the public key parts of the set of one-time-use keys arranged as leaf nodes of a hash tree, a root node of the hash tree representing a public key of a signature authority; receive a request for a number of one-time-use keys from a delegate signature authority; identify a number of unused one-time-use keys from the set of one-time-use keys based on information in a delegate database, the delegate database containing information that identifies one-time-use keys that have been assigned to delegates; provide the number of unused one-time-use keys to the delegate signature authority; and update the database to indicate that the number of unused one-time-use keys have been assigned to the delegate signature authority. 14. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to generate a one-time-use key by at least: generating a secret key part of the one-time-use key from a random number; and generating a public key part of the one-time-use key by determining a cryptographic hash of the secret key part. 15. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to: receive, from the authorized delegate, information that identifies a subset of the number of unused one-time-use keys; and update the database to indicate that the subset of the number of unused one-time-use keys are no longer assigned to the delegate signature authority. 16. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to: identify a set of internal nodes of the hash tree based on the leaf nodes that are associated with the number of unused one-time-use keys, the set of internal nodes having associated hash values that enable the delegate signature authority to re-create the root node of the hash tree from the number of unused one-time-use keys; and provide the associated hash values to the delegate signature authority on. 17. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to: determine that there are not sufficient unused one-time-use keys in the set of one-time-use keys to satisfy the request; generate an additional set of one-time-use keys having public key parts arranged as leaf nodes of an additional hash tree; and wherein a new root node of the additional hash tree represents another public key of the signature authority. 18. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to: receive, from the delegate signature authority, an indication that a one-time- use key has been used to generate a digital signature; record, in a key -use database maintained by the signature authority, that the one-time-use key has been used to generate the digital signature; and determine, based at least in part on information in the key -use database, whether the one-time-use key has been used to generate more than one different digital signature. 19. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 18, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to, based at least in part on having determined that the one-time-use key has been used to generate more than one different digital signature, revoke the one-time-use key. 20. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 13, wherein the instructions further comprise instructions that, as a result of being executed by the one or more processors, cause the computer system to: determine that there are not sufficient unused one-time-use keys in the set of one-time-use keys to satisfy the request; identify a set of reusable one-time-use keys based on information in a key -use database maintained by the signature authority; and provide the set of reusable one-time-use keys to the delegated signature authority.
